---
title: "Electric Vehicle Population Analysis"
author:
  - name: Ian Peralta  |  Evan Gill
    email: iep5058@psu.edu  |  eag5894@psu.edu
date: today
date-format: long
format:
  pdf:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    fig-pos: "H"
    geometry: margin=1in
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    fig.align: "center"
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

# Load required packages
library(tidyverse)
library(scales)
library(knitr)

# ── Data import ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Read raw CSV; show_col_types = FALSE suppresses the column-spec message.
ev_raw <- read_csv(
  "Electric_Vehicle_Population_Data.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

# ── Data wrangling ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Rename columns to snake_case for consistency, then filter and mutate.
ev <- ev_raw |>
  rename(
    vin               = `VIN (1-10)`,
    county            = County,
    city              = City,
    state             = State,
    postal_code       = `Postal Code`,
    model_year        = `Model Year`,
    make              = Make,
    model             = Model,
    ev_type           = `Electric Vehicle Type`,
    cafv_eligibility  = `Clean Alternative Fuel Vehicle (CAFV) Eligibility`,
    electric_range    = `Electric Range`,
    legislative_district = `Legislative District`,
    dol_vehicle_id    = `DOL Vehicle ID`,
    vehicle_location  = `Vehicle Location`,
    electric_utility  = `Electric Utility`,
    census_tract_2020 = `2020 Census Tract`
  ) |>
  # Remove rows missing geographic or range information
  filter(!is.na(county), !is.na(city), !is.na(electric_range)) |>
  mutate(
    # Standardise make and model capitalisation
    make       = str_to_title(make),
    model      = str_to_title(model),
    make_model = str_c(make, model, sep = " "),
    # Shorten EV-type labels for readability in plots
    ev_type_short = case_when(
      str_detect(ev_type, "BEV")  ~ "Battery EV (BEV)",
      str_detect(ev_type, "PHEV") ~ "Plug-in Hybrid EV (PHEV)",
      TRUE                        ~ ev_type
    )
  )

# ── Dataset-level summary (used inline throughout the report) ─────────────────
summary_stats <- ev |>
  summarize(
    n_records           = n(),
    n_counties          = n_distinct(county),
    n_makes             = n_distinct(make),
    earliest_model_year = min(model_year, na.rm = TRUE),
    newest_model_year   = max(model_year, na.rm = TRUE),
    pct_bev             = round(
      mean(ev_type_short == "Battery EV (BEV)") * 100, 1
    )
  )
```

\newpage
# Introduction

Washington State has been at the forefront of electric vehicle (EV) adoption
in the United States, supported by state incentives, a growing charging
infrastructure, and a population that has broadly embraced clean-energy
transportation. This report explores the Washington State Department of
Licensing EV population data to better understand where EVs are registered,
which makes and models dominate the market, how registrations have grown
over model years, and what the distribution of reported electric range looks
like.

The dataset contains `r format(summary_stats$n_records, big.mark = ",")` EV
registrations spread across `r summary_stats$n_counties` counties, covering
model years from `r summary_stats$earliest_model_year` to
`r summary_stats$newest_model_year`. Battery EVs (BEVs) make up
`r summary_stats$pct_bev`% of all registrations, with Plug-in Hybrid EVs
(PHEVs) comprising the remainder. All analyses in this report follow an
Exploratory Data Analysis structure, our goal is to surface
patterns and generate hypotheses rather than confirm specific pre-stated
claims. All coding follows the Tidyverse standard throughout, using
consistent piping with `|>` and `{ggplot2}` for visualization.

# Data

## Provenance

The data used in this report are the *Electric Vehicle Population Data*
published by the Washington State Department of Licensing (DOL). The dataset
is made publicly available through the Washington State open-data portal and
is updated on a regular basis to reflect current vehicle registrations. For
this analysis, we used the version downloaded from the United States open
data portal (data.gov).

**Citation:** Washington State Department of Licensing. (2024). *Electric
Vehicle Population* [Data set]. Washington State Open Data Portal.
https://data.wa.gov/Transportation/Electric-Vehicle-Population-Data/f6w7-q2d2

## Fair and Care Principles

The dataset satisfies the core **FAIR** principles in the following ways.
It is findable; the data carry a stable URL and a persistent identifier on
the Washington State open-data portal. It is accessible, the portal allows
direct CSV download without requiring authentication. It is interoperable, 
the file is a plain CSV with column names that map naturally to standard
transportation and geographic concepts. It is reusable, the DOL provides
field-level documentation, and the data are released under a public-domain
licence, meaning researchers may freely build upon them.

Applying the **CARE** principles, which focus on Indigenous data sovereignty,
requires more nuance. The data describes vehicle registrations, not individuals
or communities, so direct CARE concerns are less acute than in, say, health
or census data. Nevertheless, the Collective Benefit principle is relevant:
the dataset is a public resource that can inform equitable EV-infrastructure
planning for all Washington communities, including tribal nations whose
territories fall within the state. The Authority to Control principle
highlights a challenge: tribal-land registrations are aggregated into standard
county codes, which obscures any distinct EV-adoption patterns within those
communities. A fuller implementation of CARE would require the DOL to
collaborate with tribal governments on how, and at what resolution, their data
are reported.

## Cases and Attributes

Each case in this dataset is a single registered electric vehicle in
Washington State at the time of the snapshot. More precisely, a case
corresponds to one unique vehicle title on record with the DOL, identified by
a partial Vehicle Identification Number (VIN).

The data collection records the following attributes for each registered
vehicle. The county, city, state, and postal code of the registered owner
capture the geographic location of each vehicle. The model year indicates
the manufacturer's designated production year for the vehicle. The make and
model fields record the manufacturer name and specific model designation,
respectively. The electric vehicle type distinguishes between Battery EVs
, which run exclusively on electric power, and Plug-in Hybrid EVs
, which combine an electric motor with a combustion engine. The Clean
Alternative Fuel Vehicle (CAFV) eligibility field records whether the vehicle
qualifies for Washington State's clean-fuel incentive programs. The electric
range attribute reports the EPA-estimated all-electric driving range in miles;
records coded as zero typically indicate that the range has not yet been
researched by the DOL. Additional administrative attributes, the DOL vehicle
ID, legislative district, electric utility provider, and 2020 Census tract
provide context for regulatory and geographic analyses.

# Results

## Geographic Distribution of EV Registrations {#sec-counties}

```{r}
#| label: fig-top-counties
#| fig-cap: "Top 10 Washington counties by number of registered EVs. King
#|   County accounts for the majority of all registrations in the state."
#| fig-alt: "A horizontal bar chart showing the top 10 Washington counties by
#|   EV registrations. King County has by far the most with 138,268 registered
#|   EVs, followed by Snohomish at 35,136 and Pierce at 23,040. The remaining
#|   seven counties — Clark, Thurston, Kitsap, Spokane, Whatcom, Benton, and
#|   Skagit — each have fewer than 20,000 registrations, with bars decreasing
#|   in length from left to right. The x-axis is labelled 'Number of
#|   registered EVs' and scaled in units of thousands (K). The chart uses a
#|   minimal theme with horizontal gridlines removed."
#| out-width: "95%"

# ── Plot 1: Top 10 counties ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Count registrations per county, keep top 10, reorder for ranked display.
plot_top_counties <- ev |>
  count(county, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  mutate(county = fct_reorder(county, n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = county)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = comma(n)), hjust = -0.1, size = 3.5) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = label_number(scale_cut = cut_short_scale()),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.12))
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "EV County Concentration",
    x     = "Number of registered EVs",
    y     = "County"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())

plot_top_counties
```

@fig-top-counties reveals a striking geographic concentration of EV
registrations across Washington State. King County, which encompasses Seattle
and its immediate suburbs, dominates with 138,268 registered EVs, nearly four
times the count of second-place Snohomish County (35,136). Together, King and
Snohomish counties account for the majority of all registrations in the
dataset, a pattern that likely reflects the combined effect of higher median
incomes, greater population density, and more developed charging infrastructure
in the Puget Sound metro area. Counties further from the Seattle metro, such
as Benton (4,020) and Skagit (3,343), trail far behind, suggesting that EV
adoption remains heavily concentrated in urban and suburban western Washington.
This urban-rural gap may reflect both economic factors and the greater range
anxiety associated with rural driving patterns, and it hints at where targeted
infrastructure investment could have the most impact.

\newpage
## Most Common EV Makes {#sec-makes}

```{r}
#| label: tbl-top-makes
#| tbl-cap: "Top 10 EV makes registered in Washington State, ranked by total
#|   number of registrations."

# ── Summary table: top 10 makes ───────────────────────────────────────────────
# Compute counts, then format numbers with commas for the rendered table.
ev |>
  count(make, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  mutate(
    Rank = row_number(),
    `Number of Registered EVs` = format(n, big.mark = ",")
  ) |>
  select(Rank, Make = make, `Number of Registered EVs`) |>
  kable(align = c("c", "l", "r"))
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-top-makes
#| fig-cap: "Top 10 EV manufacturers by number of registered vehicles in
#|   Washington State. Tesla leads by a wide margin."
#| fig-alt: "A horizontal bar chart showing the top 10 EV manufacturers by
#|   Washington State registrations. Tesla has the longest bar at 114,808
#|   vehicles, more than five times larger than second-place Chevrolet at
#|   19,410. The remaining eight makes — Nissan, Ford, Kia, Toyota, BMW,
#|   Hyundai, Rivian, and Volkswagen — each have between roughly 7,500 and
#|   16,200 registrations, with bars decreasing in length going down the chart.
#|   The x-axis is labelled 'Number of registered EVs' and is scaled in
#|   thousands (K). The chart uses a minimal theme with horizontal gridlines
#|   removed."
#| out-width: "95%"

# ── Plot 2: Top 10 makes ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Count by make, keep top 10, reorder for ranked bar display.
plot_top_makes <- ev |>
  count(make, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  mutate(make = fct_reorder(make, n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = make)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = comma(n)), hjust = -0.1, size = 3.5) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = label_number(scale_cut = cut_short_scale()),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.12))
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Tesla Dominates EV Registrations by Make",
    x     = "Number of registered EVs",
    y     = "Make"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())

plot_top_makes
```

@tbl-top-makes and @fig-top-makes together show the remarkable degree to which
Tesla dominates EV registrations in Washington State. With 114,808 registered
vehicles, Tesla accounts for nearly half of all EVs in the dataset, a margin
that far exceeds the combined totals of Chevrolet (19,410), Nissan (16,153),
and Ford (15,422), the next three manufacturers on the list. The gap between
Tesla and every other make is not just large; it is qualitatively different in
scale. One plausible explanation is Tesla's early market entry and its
deliberate focus on building a dedicated charging network, which reduced the
range anxiety that has historically limited EV adoption. The presence of Rivian
(8,950), a relatively young EV-only startup, alongside legacy automakers
like Toyota and BMW suggests that the market is beginning to diversify, even if
Tesla's lead remains substantial.

## EV Registrations by Model Year and Type {#sec-modelyear}

```{r}
#| label: fig-model-year
#| fig-cap: "Number of registered EVs by model year (2011–2027), split by EV
#|   type. BEV registrations surge after 2020 and peak around model year 2023,
#|   while PHEV registrations grow more slowly."
#| fig-alt: "A line chart with points showing registered EV counts by model
#|   year from 2011 to 2027, with two lines: a salmon-pink line for Battery EVs
#|   (BEV) and a teal line for Plug-in Hybrid EVs (PHEV). Both lines are low
#|   and roughly parallel through 2019. Starting around 2020 the BEV line rises
#|   sharply, peaking near 50,000 registrations around model year 2023 before
#|   falling steeply toward 2027. The PHEV line increases more gradually,
#|   reaching a smaller peak around 10,000 near model year 2024 before also
#|   declining. The x-axis shows model year and the y-axis shows number of
#|   registered EVs scaled in thousands (K). A legend at the top distinguishes
#|   the two EV types by both colour and point shape."
#| out-width: "95%"

# ── Plot 3: Registrations by model year and EV type ───────────────────────────
# Filter to a meaningful model-year window; count by year and type.
plot_model_year_type <- ev |>
  filter(model_year >= 2011, model_year <= 2027) |>
  count(model_year, ev_type_short) |>
  ggplot(aes(
    x     = model_year,
    y     = n,
    color = ev_type_short,
    shape = ev_type_short
  )) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = label_number(scale_cut = cut_short_scale())
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "BEV registrations surge after 2020",
    x     = "Model year",
    y     = "Number of registered EVs",
    color = "EV type",
    shape = "EV type"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

plot_model_year_type
```

@fig-model-year shows how EV registrations have evolved across model years
and between the two drivetrain types. Both BEV and PHEV registrations remained
modest and roughly parallel through 2019, then the BEV line diverges sharply
upward, consistent with Tesla's rapid production expansion and the broader
industry shift toward fully electric platforms. The BEV line peaks near model
year 2023 and then drops steeply, which most likely reflects a data-collection
artefact: newer model years are simply underrepresented because vehicles take
time to be titled and recorded after purchase, so the apparent decline toward
2026–2027 should not be interpreted as falling EV sales. PHEVs follow a
similar but much flatter trajectory, suggesting that while the plug-in hybrid
segment is growing, it has not kept pace with the surge in fully electric
vehicles. The divergence between the two lines after 2020 is one of the
clearest signals in the data that Washington drivers have been moving decisively
toward full electrification.

## Most Common EV Models {#sec-models}

```{r}
#| label: fig-top-models
#| fig-cap: "Top 10 make-and-model combinations by number of registrations.
#|   Tesla models occupy four of the top six spots."
#| fig-alt: "A horizontal bar chart showing the top 10 EV make-and-model
#|   combinations by Washington State registrations. Tesla Model Y has the
#|   longest bar at 60,356 vehicles, followed by Tesla Model 3 at 37,835. Third
#|   place goes to Nissan Leaf at 13,499, followed by Tesla Model S at 7,734
#|   and Chevrolet Bolt EV at 7,701. The remaining five models — Tesla Model X,
#|   Ford Mustang Mach-E, Hyundai Ioniq 5, Volkswagen ID.4, and Rivian R1S —
#|   each have between roughly 5,000 and 7,000 registrations. The x-axis is
#|   labelled 'Number of registered EVs' and is scaled in thousands (K). The
#|   chart uses a minimal theme with horizontal gridlines removed."
#| out-width: "95%"

# ── Plot 4: Top 10 make-model combinations ────────────────────────────────────
# Combine make and model into a single label; count and reorder.
plot_top_models <- ev |>
  count(make_model, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  mutate(make_model = fct_reorder(make_model, n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = make_model)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = comma(n)), hjust = -0.1, size = 3.5) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = label_number(scale_cut = cut_short_scale()),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.12))
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Top Models are Mostly BEVs",
    x     = "Number of registered EVs",
    y     = "Make and model"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())

plot_top_models
```

@fig-top-models reinforces the manufacturer-level picture from @sec-makes,
now at model resolution. The Tesla Model Y (60,356) and Tesla Model 3 (37,835)
together account for nearly half of all registrations in the dataset, an
extraordinary concentration in just two models. The Nissan Leaf (13,499)
occupies a distant third place, reflecting its status as one of the earliest
mass-market EVs available in the United States. Notably, four of the top six
spots belong to Tesla, confirming that the brand's dominance is not spread
across many niche models but is driven by two high-volume, mainstream
offerings. The presence of the Ford Mustang Mach-E, Hyundai Ioniq 5, and
Volkswagen ID.4 in the lower portion of the chart suggests that non-Tesla BEV
alternatives are gaining traction, even if they have not yet approached
Tesla's volumes.

## Distribution of Electric Range {#sec-range}

```{r}
#| label: fig-range
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of reported electric range (miles) among Washington
#|   State EVs with a recorded range greater than zero. The dashed vertical line
#|   marks the median range. Two clusters are visible: a lower cluster centred
#|   near 20–40 miles (typical of PHEVs) and an upper cluster near 200–260
#|   miles (typical of BEVs)."
#| fig-alt: "A histogram showing the distribution of electric range in miles
#|   for Washington State EVs with range greater than zero. The x-axis is
#|   labelled 'Electric range, miles' and spans from 0 to just above 300. The
#|   y-axis is labelled 'Number of registered EVs' and is scaled in thousands.
#|   A vertical dashed line marks the median. The distribution is clearly
#|   bimodal: the tallest bars appear in the 20-to-40-mile range with counts
#|   near 13,000 to 18,000, representing short-range plug-in hybrids; a second
#|   cluster appears between roughly 200 and 270 miles with counts up to about
#|   10,000, representing long-range battery EVs. The gap between the two
#|   clusters, from roughly 120 to 190 miles, contains very few vehicles."
#| out-width: "95%"

# ── Plot 5: Electric range histogram ─────────────────────────────────────────
# Exclude range == 0 (coded as unknown by DOL); add median reference line.
plot_range <- ev |>
  filter(electric_range > 0) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = electric_range)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 10, color = "white") +
  geom_vline(
    aes(xintercept = median(electric_range)),
    linetype  = "dashed",
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = label_number(scale_cut = cut_short_scale())
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Electric Range is Bimodal Overall",
    subtitle = "Records with range = 0 (unknown) excluded",
    x        = "Electric range, miles",
    y        = "Number of registered EVs"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

plot_range
```

@fig-range reveals a distinctly bimodal distribution of electric range across
the Washington EV population. A first cluster of vehicles is concentrated in the
20-to-40-mile range, these are almost certainly PHEVs, whose all-electric mode
is designed for short urban trips rather than long-distance travel. A second,
broader cluster appears between roughly 200 and 260 miles, representing the
mainstream BEV segment where most modern Tesla and non-Tesla fully electric
vehicles fall. The gap between the two clusters, roughly 120 to 190 miles,
contains very few vehicles, suggesting that manufacturers have largely
converged on two distinct design philosophies rather than building vehicles
that split the difference. The dashed line marking the median sits in the
lower-range cluster, which reflects the large number of PHEV registrations
pulling the central tendency downward. A natural follow-up question would be
whether this bimodal structure has shifted over time as BEV ranges have
improved.

## Make Distribution Across Top Counties {#sec-heatmap}

```{r}
#| label: fig-heatmap
#| fig-cap: "Heatmap of EV registrations for the top 6 makes across the top 6
#|   Washington counties. Cell colour encodes EV count; exact counts are
#|   printed within each cell."
#| fig-alt: "A tile heatmap with county on the y-axis (King, Snohomish,
#|   Pierce, Clark, Thurston, Kitsap from top to bottom) and EV make on the
#|   x-axis (Tesla, Chevrolet, Nissan, Ford, Kia, Toyota from left to right).
#|   Cell fill ranges from dark navy (low counts) to light blue (high counts).
#|   The Tesla-King County cell is the brightest, showing 59.872K vehicles and
#|   stands out sharply from all other cells. All other cells are dark,
#|   indicating much lower counts. Snohomish-Tesla shows 16.717K, Pierce-Tesla
#|   shows 8.941K. Counts for non-Tesla makes across all counties are generally
#|   below 8K. A legend on the right maps fill colour to EV count in units of
#|   thousands (K)."
#| out-width: "95%"

# ── Plot 6: County × make heatmap ─────────────────────────────────────────────
# Identify the top 6 counties and top 6 makes independently, then cross-tabulate.
top_county_names <- ev |>
  count(county, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_head(n = 6) |>
  pull(county)

top_make_names <- ev |>
  count(make, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_head(n = 6) |>
  pull(make)

plot_county_make_heatmap <- ev |>
  filter(county %in% top_county_names, make %in% top_make_names) |>
  count(county, make) |>
  mutate(
    county = factor(county, levels = rev(top_county_names)),
    make   = factor(make,   levels = top_make_names)
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = make, y = county, fill = n)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = label_number(scale_cut = cut_short_scale())(n)),
    size = 3.2
  ) +
  scale_fill_continuous(
    labels = label_number(scale_cut = cut_short_scale())
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Teslas Concentrated in King County",
    x     = "Make",
    y     = "County",
    fill  = "EV count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank())

plot_county_make_heatmap
```

@fig-heatmap brings together the geographic and manufacturer threads from
earlier sections. The most immediate feature is the Tesla-King County cell,
which at nearly 60,000 vehicles is so much larger than every other cell that
it dominates the color scale, leaving the rest of the heatmap in uniformly
dark shades. This visual asymmetry highlights how extreme the concentration
of Tesla registrations in King County is relative to the rest of the matrix.
Snohomish County's Tesla cell (16.7K) is the second brightest, consistent with
its large overall registration count seen in @fig-top-counties. For non-Tesla
makes, counts are far more uniform across counties, Chevrolet, Nissan, Ford,
Kia, and Toyota each show counts in the hundreds to low thousands across all
six counties, without the sharp drop-off that characterizes Tesla. This pattern
suggests that while Tesla's dominance is geographically concentrated in the
Seattle metro, competing makes have a somewhat more even geographic footprint.

\newpage
# Conclusion

Taken together, these six visualizations paint a consistent picture of EV
adoption in Washington State. Registrations are geographically concentrated in
the Puget Sound metro area, particularly King County. Tesla has an unmatched
presence at both the manufacturer and model level, driven primarily by the
Model Y and Model 3. The population has grown rapidly in recent model years, with
BEVs outpacing PHEVs by an increasing margin since 2020. And the bimodal
electric-range distribution reflects the coexistence of two
different vehicle philosophies, short-range hybrids and long-range pure
electrics, within the same registered group. Future analyses could examine
whether the geographic and brand concentration observed here is narrowing over
time as EV options expand and charging infrastructure reaches more rural parts
of the state.

\newpage

# GenAI Usage Appendix {.unnumbered}

This appendix discloses all use of generative AI tools in the development of
this report, in accordance with course policy and Open Science principles.

**Tool used:** Anthropic Claude (Sonnet 4.6)

**Date of use:** April 2025

**Purpose:** Generating alt text and long descriptions for all six figures
in this report (@fig-top-counties, @fig-top-makes, @fig-model-year,
@fig-top-models, @fig-range, @fig-heatmap).

**Prompts used (one per figure, representative example shown for
@fig-top-counties):**

*Prompt:* "Write alt text and a long description for
the following bar chart. The chart shows
the top 10 Washington counties by number of registered EVs. Be specific about
visual features, axis labels, and notable patterns."

**Response (Claude, used as basis for alt text in @fig-top-counties):**
Claude returned a description noting King County's dominant bar at 138,268
vehicles, the four-times gap between King and Snohomish, the decreasing bar
lengths for the remaining counties, and the K-scaled x-axis labelled
"Number of registered EVs." We edited this response for length and integrated
it into the `fig-alt` chunk option for each figure. The same prompt structure
was applied to each of the remaining five figures, with the specific chart
image provided each time.

\newpage

# Code Appendix {.unnumbered}

## `00_ev_data_prep.R` {.unnumbered}

```{r}
# Author: Ian
# Reviewer: Evan
# Description: Loads, cleans, and prepares EV dataset for analysis. Renames variables,
# filters missing values, and creates derived variables used across all visualizations.

#| label: appendix-data-prep
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: false

# ── Libraries ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
library(tidyverse)
library(scales)

# ── Data import ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Read raw CSV; show_col_types = FALSE suppresses the column-spec message.
ev_raw <- read_csv(
  "Electric_Vehicle_Population_Data.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

# ── Data wrangling ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Rename columns to snake_case for consistency, then filter and mutate.
ev <- ev_raw |>
  rename(
    vin               = `VIN (1-10)`,
    county            = County,
    city              = City,
    state             = State,
    postal_code       = `Postal Code`,
    model_year        = `Model Year`,
    make              = Make,
    model             = Model,
    ev_type           = `Electric Vehicle Type`,
    cafv_eligibility  = `Clean Alternative Fuel Vehicle (CAFV) Eligibility`,
    electric_range    = `Electric Range`,
    legislative_district = `Legislative District`,
    dol_vehicle_id    = `DOL Vehicle ID`,
    vehicle_location  = `Vehicle Location`,
    electric_utility  = `Electric Utility`,
    census_tract_2020 = `2020 Census Tract`
  ) |>
  # Remove rows missing geographic or range information
  filter(!is.na(county), !is.na(city), !is.na(electric_range)) |>
  mutate(
    # Standardise make and model capitalisation
    make       = str_to_title(make),
    model      = str_to_title(model),
    make_model = str_c(make, model, sep = " "),
    # Shorten EV-type labels for readability in plots
    ev_type_short = case_when(
      str_detect(ev_type, "BEV")  ~ "Battery EV (BEV)",
      str_detect(ev_type, "PHEV") ~ "Plug-in Hybrid EV (PHEV)",
      TRUE                        ~ ev_type
    )
  )

# ── Dataset-level summary ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
summary_stats <- ev |>
  summarize(
    n_records           = n(),
    n_counties          = n_distinct(county),
    n_makes             = n_distinct(make),
    earliest_model_year = min(model_year, na.rm = TRUE),
    newest_model_year   = max(model_year, na.rm = TRUE),
    pct_bev             = round(
      mean(ev_type_short == "Battery EV (BEV)") * 100, 1
    )
  )
```

\newpage

## `01_top_counties.R` {.unnumbered}

```{r}
# Author: Evan
# Reviewer: Ian
# Description: Creates bar chart of top 10 counties by EV registrations to analyze geographic concentration.

#| label: appendix-top-counties
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: false

# ── Plot 1: Top 10 counties ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Count registrations per county, keep top 10, reorder for ranked display.
plot_top_counties <- ev |>
  count(county, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  mutate(county = fct_reorder(county, n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = county)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = comma(n)), hjust = -0.1, size = 3.5) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = label_number(scale_cut = cut_short_scale()),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.12))
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "EV registrations concentrate in a few counties",
    x     = "Number of registered EVs",
    y     = "County"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())

ggsave(
  "figures/01_top_counties.png",
  plot_top_counties,
  width  = 9,
  height = 5.4,
  dpi    = 300
)
```

\newpage

## `02_top_makes.R` {.unnumbered}

```{r}
# Author: Ian
# Reviewer: Evan
# Description: Creates bar chart of top EV makes to identify dominant manufacturers in the dataset.

#| label: appendix-top-makes
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: false

# ── Plot 2: Top 10 makes ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Count by make, keep top 10, reorder for ranked bar display.
plot_top_makes <- ev |>
  count(make, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  mutate(make = fct_reorder(make, n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = make)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = comma(n)), hjust = -0.1, size = 3.5) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = label_number(scale_cut = cut_short_scale()),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.12))
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Tesla dominates EV registrations by make",
    x     = "Number of registered EVs",
    y     = "Make"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())

ggsave(
  "figures/02_top_makes.png",
  plot_top_makes,
  width  = 9,
  height = 5.4,
  dpi    = 300
)
```

\newpage

## `03_model_year_by_type.R` {.unnumbered}

```{r}
# Author: Evan
# Reviewer: Ian
# Description: Creates time series plot of EV registrations by model year and EV type (BEV vs PHEV).

#| label: appendix-model-year
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: false

# ── Plot 3: Registrations by model year and EV type ───────────────────────────
# Filter to a meaningful model-year window; count by year and type.
plot_model_year_type <- ev |>
  filter(model_year >= 2011, model_year <= 2027) |>
  count(model_year, ev_type_short) |>
  ggplot(aes(
    x     = model_year,
    y     = n,
    color = ev_type_short,
    shape = ev_type_short
  )) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = label_number(scale_cut = cut_short_scale())
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "BEV registrations surge after 2020",
    x     = "Model year",
    y     = "Number of registered EVs",
    color = "EV type",
    shape = "EV type"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

ggsave(
  "figures/03_model_year_by_type.png",
  plot_model_year_type,
  width  = 9,
  height = 5.4,
  dpi    = 300
)
```

\newpage

## `04_top_models.R` {.unnumbered}

```{r}
# Author: Ian
# Reviewer: Evan
# Description: Creates bar chart of most common EV make and model combinations to analyze market leaders.

#| label: appendix-top-models
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: false

# ── Plot 4: Top 10 make-model combinations ────────────────────────────────────
# Combine make and model into a single label; count and reorder.
plot_top_models <- ev |>
  count(make_model, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  mutate(make_model = fct_reorder(make_model, n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = make_model)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = comma(n)), hjust = -0.1, size = 3.5) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = label_number(scale_cut = cut_short_scale()),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.12))
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Top models are mostly BEVs",
    x     = "Number of registered EVs",
    y     = "Make and model"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())

ggsave(
  "figures/04_top_models.png",
  plot_top_models,
  width  = 9,
  height = 5.4,
  dpi    = 300
)
```

\newpage

## `05_range_histogram.R` {.unnumbered}

```{r}
# Author: Evan
# Reviewer: Ian
# Description: Creates histogram of EV electric range (excluding 0 values) to examine distribution of driving range.

#| label: appendix-range
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: false

# ── Plot 5: Electric range histogram ─────────────────────────────────────────
# Exclude range == 0 (coded as unknown by DOL); add median reference line.
plot_range <- ev |>
  filter(electric_range > 0) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = electric_range)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 10, color = "white") +
  geom_vline(
    aes(xintercept = median(electric_range)),
    linetype  = "dashed",
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = label_number(scale_cut = cut_short_scale())
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Electric range is bimodal across the fleet",
    subtitle = "Records with range = 0 (unknown) excluded",
    x        = "Electric range, miles",
    y        = "Number of registered EVs"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

ggsave(
  "figures/05_range_histogram.png",
  plot_range,
  width  = 9,
  height = 5.4,
  dpi    = 300
)
```

\newpage

## `06_county_make_heatmap.R` {.unnumbered}

```{r}
# Author: Ian
# Reviewer: Evan
# Description: Creates heatmap of EV counts by county and make to explore how manufacturers are distributed geographically.


#| label: appendix-heatmap
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: false

# ── Plot 6: County x make heatmap ─────────────────────────────────────────────
# Identify top 6 counties and top 6 makes independently, then cross-tabulate.
top_county_names <- ev |>
  count(county, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_head(n = 6) |>
  pull(county)

top_make_names <- ev |>
  count(make, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_head(n = 6) |>
  pull(make)

plot_county_make_heatmap <- ev |>
  filter(county %in% top_county_names, make %in% top_make_names) |>
  count(county, make) |>
  mutate(
    county = factor(county, levels = rev(top_county_names)),
    make   = factor(make,   levels = top_make_names)
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = make, y = county, fill = n)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = label_number(scale_cut = cut_short_scale())(n)),
    size = 3.2
  ) +
  scale_fill_continuous(
    labels = label_number(scale_cut = cut_short_scale())
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Tesla's dominance is concentrated in King County",
    x     = "Make",
    y     = "County",
    fill  = "EV count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank())

ggsave(
  "figures/06_county_make_heatmap.png",
  plot_county_make_heatmap,
  width  = 9,
  height = 5.4,
  dpi    = 300
)
```
